This makes "issue/PR only changes runtime templates" a hard-bounded no-op rather than a prompt-dependent classification. The all-paths check is required: a diff containing a runtime template plus any non-prompt source/test file, any non-LLM prompt, or a rename from a real prompt to a runtime prompt must NOT short-circuit, otherwise real changes would be silently skipped. Returns False on main/master/HEAD, when any non-runtime-template path is present, or on error so the LLM fallback still runs for those cases.
   c. LLM fallback (only if both (a) and (b) returned empty/False): build LLM prompt using `load_prompt_template("agentic_sync_identify_modules_LLM")` with `{issue_content}` and `{architecture_json}` placeholders. Call `run_agentic_task()` to identify modules and validate dependencies.
8. Parse LLM response for `MODULES_TO_SYNC: [...]`, `DEPS_VALID: true|false`, and optional `DEPS_CORRECTIONS: [...]`. Deduplicate modules in the list preserving first-occurrence order. Return failure `"LLM identified no modules to sync"` if the LLM returns an empty module list and the branch diff is not verifiably runtime-template-only; generic empty LLM output must not become a silent no-op.
9. Strip language suffixes from basenames using `extract_module_from_include(m + ".prompt")` — LLM returns architecture.json filenames (e.g. "crm_models_Python") but pdd sync expects bare basenames (e.g. "crm_models"). After stripping, deduplicate again. Then drop any basename for which `_is_runtime_llm_template(basename)` returns True — runtime `*_LLM.prompt` templates have no language-suffixed sync companion and must never reach `_filter_invalid_basenames` or dry-run. Log one INFO line per drop when `not quiet`.
10. Filter hallucinated basenames via `_filter_invalid_basenames(modules, architecture)`: validate each basename exists in architecture.json (using `extract_module_from_include` for standard filenames; fallback stripping is performed only for non-prompt code-file extensions such as `.tsx`, `.ts`, `.jsx`, `.js`, `.py`, and a generic `.prompt`). **Architecture entries whose `filename` ends in `_LLM.prompt` are skipped entirely when building `known_basenames`** — runtime LLM templates must not contribute either the bare `_LLM` basename or a stripped owner-style basename, otherwise hallucinated `<basename>_LLM` (or stripped `<basename>`) inputs would be marked valid. As a hard boundary, this filter MUST also reject any basename for which `_is_runtime_llm_template(basename)` returns True, even if a `*_LLM.prompt` entry is present in architecture.json — those entries are runtime templates, not syncable modules. Fail if all basenames are invalid.
11. If LLM flags dependency corrections: update architecture.json in-place before building the graph when `dry_run=False`; when `dry_run=True`, report that corrections were suggested but do not write architecture.json
12. Build dependency subgraph from the in-memory combined architecture data via `build_dep_graph_from_architecture_data(architecture, modules_to_sync, source_name=...)`, which returns `DepGraphFromArchitectureResult(graph=..., warnings=...)`. This must use the same builder as `run_global_sync` so issue sync and global sync produce consistent dependency ordering and preserve nested-architecture edges (Phase 0 of #1328). Use **only** `result.graph` as `dep_graph` for the runner. If `result.warnings` is non-empty and **`not quiet`**, print each warning as a **yellow** line prefixed with `Warning:` (Rich yellow styling) so operators see orphan deps and partial-sync omitted edges.
13. Fallback: if no architecture.json, use `build_dependency_graph(prompts_dir)` from `sync_order.py` (no architecture warnings in that path)
14. Run dry-run validation for each module (see Dry-Run Validation below); fail the workflow if any module cannot be validated
15. Filter already-synced modules via fingerprint check (see Fingerprint Filtering below); skip workflow if all modules are synced
16. If `dry_run=True`, stop after dry-run validation and fingerprint filtering, return success with a concise list of modules that would sync, and do not instantiate `AsyncSyncRunner` or `DurableSyncRunner`
17. Dispatch to `AsyncSyncRunner` with basenames, dep graph, sync options, GitHub info, `issue_url`, `module_cwds` map from dry-run validation, and `initial_cost` (the sum of LLM identification + dry-run LLM fallback costs) so the runner can display the full cost in its GitHub progress comment
18. If `durable=True`, dispatch to `DurableSyncRunner` instead of `AsyncSyncRunner`. Pass the issue URL, durable branch override, `no_resume` flag, and durable max-parallel setting through unchanged. Durable mode must still use the same module identification, dependency graph, dry-run validation, fingerprint filtering, sync options, and initial cost accounting as standard issue sync.
19. Aggregate costs from LLM identification + dry-run LLM fallback + runner execution
20. Include `one_session`, `local`, and `target_coverage` in `sync_options` dict passed to the selected runner

% Global Sync (Tier 1)
1. Function: `run_global_sync(*, verbose: bool = False, quiet: bool = False, budget: Optional[float] = None, skip_verify: bool = False, skip_tests: bool = False, agentic_mode: bool = True, no_steer: bool = True, max_attempts: Optional[int] = None, dry_run: bool = False, target_coverage: Optional[float] = None, one_session: bool = False, local: bool = False, timeout_adder: float = 0.0, strength: Optional[float] = None, temperature: Optional[float] = None, context_override: Optional[str] = None) -> Tuple[bool, str, float, str]`. Forward `timeout_adder`, `strength`, `temperature`, and `context_override` to the runner via `sync_options`.
2. Load scoped architecture modules by discovering architecture.json files with `find_architecture_for_project(project_root)`, preserving each entry's architecture path and cwd. For each architecture entry, derive its cwd by calling `_resolve_module_cwd(basename, arch_path.parent)` so that any nested `.pddrc` whose context claims that basename wins over the arch file's own directory; when no nested `.pddrc` claims the basename, `_resolve_module_cwd` falls back to `arch_path.parent` so nested-architecture modules retain arch-dir isolation. Ignore nested `.pddrc` candidates under hidden/tooling directories such as `.worktrees`, `.git`, `.pdd`, `__pycache__`, `node_modules`, `venv`, and `env` so branch worktrees and caches cannot steal module cwd ownership. Fail clearly if no architecture data exists.
3. Extract syncable basenames from architecture `filename` values. Preserve subdirectory prefixes, e.g. `commands/maintenance_python.prompt` becomes `commands/maintenance`; skip non-syncable filenames such as `_LLM.prompt`. If the same basename appears in more than one architecture scope, use a display key of `<relative-architecture-dir>:<basename>` based on `arch_path.parent` for planning and dependency graph keys while preserving the plain basename as the executable sync target. Do not key duplicates by resolved cwd, because different architecture files may legitimately resolve to the same nested `.pddrc` cwd. If multiple architecture entries resolve to the same output `filepath` (relative filepaths are resolved against that entry's architecture.json directory), schedule only the first entry but preserve aliases for every skipped duplicate as `(architecture_path, basename)` pairs and sync candidates as `(basename, cwd)` pairs. Global sync analysis must evaluate every prompt-backed sync candidate in order until it finds a candidate requiring Tier 1 sync, then use that candidate's basename/cwd as the executable target; a clean first candidate must not hide a stale later duplicate, and a kept root-style basename with no prompt must not hide a syncable nested duplicate. Merge dependency edges from later duplicate-output entries into the kept entry for reporting, but also preserve each duplicate's original dependency scope so graph construction resolves those dependencies in the architecture file where they were declared. If the kept entry has a malformed non-list `dependencies` field and a skipped duplicate has valid dependency edges, keep the valid duplicate edges instead of dropping them.
4. For each scoped architecture module, resolve context/prompts_dir from that module's resolved cwd (the cwd returned by `_resolve_module_cwd`, which may be a nested `.pddrc` directory rather than `arch_path.parent`), then call `sync_determine_operation(..., log_mode=True, read_only=True, ...)` from that cwd for each detected language. Suppress info-level logs from `pdd.sync_determine_operation` around the read-only calls so global dry-run output stays readable and metadata files are never changed during analysis.
5. Analysis scope is Tier 1 prompt staleness only: queue modules whose read-only decision is `generate` or `auto-deps`; treat `nothing`/`all_synced` as clean; skip non-Tier-1 decisions such as `example`, `test`, `fix`, `update`, `verify`, or `crash` so no-argument global sync does not expand into example/test/fix workflows. Skip entries with no syncable prompt file and record them in the `skipped_modules` list for the dry-run roll-up (see Req 7). These out-of-Tier-1 entries are informational, not actionable warnings: in default dry-run output they MUST NOT be emitted as one `[yellow]Warning:[/yellow]` line per module — that per-module loop is the noise the operator sees today (issue #1016).
6. Build a dependency subgraph over scoped global-sync keys. Dep resolution proceeds in two passes per declared dependency: (a) prefer a module in the same `architecture.json` as the depending module (same `architecture_path.resolve()`), including duplicate-output aliases retained from skipped entries; (b) if no same-arch match exists, fall back to a global lookup keyed by basename across all loaded module aliases - an unambiguous match (exactly one module across all archs with that basename) is accepted so cross-arch edges resolve as the prior combined-architecture builder did, while an ambiguous cross-arch basename (claimed by multiple modules) emits an `"ambiguous cross-arch dependency"` warning and drops the edge rather than guessing. For duplicate-output modules, resolve each dependency group against the architecture path where that group was originally declared, not the kept entry's architecture path. Order queued modules with `topological_sort`; warn for dependencies omitted because they are outside the current Tier 1 target set, and also warn for unresolved dependencies whose filename matches no module in any loaded architecture scope (typo'd or stale dep edges in architecture.json) so dry-run output never silently swallows dangling edges.
7. `dry_run=True`: print a concise plan via `_print_global_sync_plan(analysis, ordered_modules, dep_warnings, budget, verbose=verbose)`. The default (non-verbose) plan MUST include:
   - number of scanned architecture modules,
   - stale module count (rendered in green when zero so success is visually unambiguous — see Req 10 below),
   - estimated cost,
   - dependency-ordered module list,
   - architecture graph warnings from `_build_scoped_global_dep_graph` printed individually in yellow (these are actionable: ambiguous/unresolved cross-arch edges),
   - a single compact roll-up line summarising `analysis.skipped_modules` bucketed by reason (e.g. `Out of Tier 1 scope: 42 example, 31 test, 18 verify, 12 update, 74 no-prompt fixture`) instead of one `[yellow]Warning:[/yellow]` per skipped entry. Bucket categories are derived from the operation token in each skipped entry (`example`, `test`, `verify`, `update`, `fix`, `crash`) plus a `no-prompt fixture` bucket for entries flagged as having no syncable prompt; unknown reasons fall into an `other` bucket. Render the roll-up as a single dim/informational line (not `[yellow]Warning:[/yellow]`) and omit the line entirely when `analysis.skipped_modules` is empty. Categories with zero count are dropped from the line; bucket order is the fixed canonical order above so test output is stable.
   When `verbose=True`, restore the previous per-module behaviour by emitting one `[yellow]Warning: <entry>[/yellow]` line for every entry in `analysis.skipped_modules` after the compact roll-up, so operators can opt back into the full enumeration for debugging.
   Do not run `AsyncSyncRunner`; return success with zero cost and model `global-sync`.
8. `dry_run=False`: if no modules are queued, return success `"All modules are already synced — nothing to do."`; otherwise reject execution when estimated global cost exceeds the user-supplied total budget, then run `AsyncSyncRunner` with `github_info=None`, `issue_url=None`, ordered scoped keys, dependency graph, module cwds, `module_targets` mapping scoped keys to executable basenames, and forwarded sync options, including `local` so child sync subprocesses preserve top-level `--local`, and `target_coverage` so child syncs use the same coverage threshold as planning. Pass the user budget as `total_budget`, not as a repeated per-module child budget.
9. Keep no-argument global sync v1 strictly scoped to prompt/code sync. Do not inspect PRD files, `prd_files`, architecture schema migrations, PRD fingerprints, or Tier 2 PRD-analysis sync. `pdd sync architecture.json` is not a global-sync alias in v1; with a positional value, it remains single-module sync input.
10. **Success styling for the analysis summary (issue #1016):** the `Global sync analysis: N stale module(s), M already synced or skipped.` line emitted from `_analyze_global_sync_modules` MUST render the stale-count fragment in green when `N == 0` (e.g. `Global sync analysis: [green]0 stale module(s)[/green], M already synced or skipped.`) so the success signal is visually unambiguous. When `N > 0`, the line is rendered with the existing neutral/bold styling — green is reserved for the zero-stale success state.

% Dry-Run Validation
Before dispatching to the runner, validate each module can actually sync:
0. **Runtime-template pre-check (hard boundary):** Before invoking any subprocess or LLM fallback, drop any basename for which `_is_runtime_llm_template(basename)` returns True. These are runtime `*_LLM.prompt` templates with no language-suffixed sync companion; calling `pdd sync` on them is guaranteed to fail with `"No prompt files found for basename '<basename>_LLM'"`. Skipping them at this layer prevents wasted subprocess invocations and LLM fallback cost. If filtering empties the list, return success with `"All modules are already synced — nothing to do."` (the runtime templates are already consumed directly by their owning code modules).
1. `_resolve_module_cwd(basename, project_root)`: determine correct cwd by scanning .pddrc files using nearest-config-wins resolution. First check whether the project root has an exact prompt for the basename. Then scan subdirectories (max depth 2, deepest match wins) for nested .pddrc files whose context patterns match the basename. Skip hidden/tooling directories (`.worktrees`, `.git`, `.pdd`, `__pycache__`, `node_modules`, `venv`, `env`, etc.) while scanning so temporary worktrees and dependency folders cannot become authoritative project contexts. Skip catch-all pattern matches (`**`, `*`) from subdirectories via `_is_catchall_match`. Nested matches from broad basename-only globs such as `*llm*` are only authoritative when the root does not already have an exact prompt and the nested prompts directory contains an exact prompt file for the requested basename (for example `prompts/llm_model_*.prompt`); structured path patterns containing `/`, exact names, or prompts_dir prefix matches remain authoritative without this extra prompt-existence check. If a nested .pddrc matches, return its directory as cwd. Only fall back to root .pddrc / project_root when no nested .pddrc claims the module. Uses `_detect_context_from_basename` and `_load_pddrc_config` from `construct_paths`.
2. `_run_single_dry_run(basename, cwd)`: run `pdd --force sync <basename> --dry-run --agentic --no-steer` as subprocess with 60s timeout and env `PDD_FORCE=1, CI=1`. Return (success, stderr).
3. After any dry-run success (programmatic cwd or LLM-resolved cwd), run a deterministic prompt-contract preflight for that module before accepting it:
   - `_prompt_contract_errors_for_module(basename, cwd, project_root)` loads the matching architecture entry, resolves the prompt/output paths, and calls `validate_prompt_contract_context`.
   - Use architecture aliases from `agentic_sync_runner` so either prompt filename basenames or output filepath basenames match.
   - Pass `require_prompt_local_source_context=True` only when `_prompt_contract_strict_self_context_required(prompt_path, root)` says the prompt file changed relative to git status or the merge base with main. This stages the no-self-include hard failure for newly changed/generated prompt contracts while keeping existing first-party legacy prompts syncable.
   - If the preflight reports errors, treat validation as failed with `"prompt contract preflight failed"` and do not invoke the LLM fallback for that module. The fallback only resolves cwd/command errors; it cannot make an inconsistent prompt/code contract safe.
4. On dry-run failure, invoke `_llm_fix_dry_run_failure` as fallback: load `agentic_sync_fix_dry_run_LLM` prompt template with project tree (2 levels), .pddrc locations, and the error. Run the LLM-suggested command via shell, extract effective cwd using a pwd marker appended to the command. Validate resolved cwd is within project root. If it succeeds, still run the prompt-contract preflight before accepting the cwd.
5. `_run_dry_run_validation(modules, project_root)`: orchestrates the above per module, returns (all_valid, module_to_cwd_map, errors, total_llm_cost).

% Fingerprint Filtering
After dry-run validation, check which modules are already fully synced:
1. `_filter_already_synced(modules, module_cwds, quiet)`: for each module, use `sync_determine_operation` in log_mode/read-only mode to check if operation is `nothing` or `all_synced` (all hashes match / workflow complete). Modules returning only no-op operations for ALL languages are filtered out.
2. Detect context and prompts_dir using nearest-config-wins .pddrc resolution: for each module, attempt to find the nearest .pddrc by searching from the module's resolved cwd (which may be a nested directory) via `_find_pddrc_file`, then use `_load_pddrc_config` and `_detect_context_from_basename`. When a module's cwd points to a subdirectory with its own .pddrc, that nested config takes precedence over the root .pddrc.
3. Discover languages via `_detect_languages_with_context(basename, prompts_dir, context_name)`
4. For each language, call `sync_determine_operation(basename, language, target_coverage=90.0, log_mode=True, read_only=True, prompts_dir=str(prompts_dir), context_override=context_name)`. If ANY language has an operation outside `nothing`/`all_synced`, keep the module.
5. Return only modules that need syncing. If list is empty, workflow returns early with success message.

% Helper Functions
- `_is_github_issue_url(s: str) -> bool`: regex check for `github\.com/.+/issues/\d+`
- `_is_runtime_llm_template(basename: str) -> bool`: return True if `basename` (or its final `/`-separated segment) ends with the case-sensitive suffix `_LLM`. Runtime LLM templates (e.g. `agentic_sync_identify_modules_LLM`, `agentic_split_step4_propose_options_LLM`) are consumed directly by their owning code module via `load_prompt_template(...)` and have no language-suffixed sync companion, so `pdd sync` cannot operate on them. Used as a shared safety predicate at three independent layers: post-LLM strip (Req 9), `_filter_invalid_basenames` (Req 10), and dry-run pre-check (Dry-Run Validation step 0). Match is case-sensitive on the final path segment to avoid false positives on legitimate basenames containing lowercase `llm`.
- `_detect_modules_from_branch_diff(project_root: Path) -> List[str]`: detect modules from git diff against main (changed .prompt files, excluding _LLM.prompt)
- `_branch_diff_is_runtime_llm_only(project_root: Path) -> bool`: return True iff **every** file path in the branch diff vs. main is a runtime `*_LLM.prompt` template under a `prompts/` directory, with at least one such path present. Use `git diff --name-status --diff-filter=ACMRD` and evaluate both old and new paths for rename/copy records. Used to short-circuit `run_agentic_sync` to a deterministic no-op when an issue/PR only modifies runtime templates (issue #1396), avoiding an unnecessary identify-modules LLM call. Returns False on main/master/HEAD, when the diff is empty, when any non-runtime-template file/path (non-prompt code/tests/configs, non-LLM prompts, or real-prompt-to-runtime rename sources) is present, or on error — so real code/test changes are not silently skipped.
- `_filter_invalid_basenames(modules: List[str], architecture: Optional[List]) -> Tuple[List[str], List[str]]`: validate basenames against architecture.json entries, return (valid, invalid). Treats runtime `*_LLM.prompt` basenames (per `_is_runtime_llm_template`) as invalid even when an architecture.json entry exists for them.
- `_find_project_root(start: Path) -> Path`: walk up to find `.pddrc` or `.git`
- `_load_architecture_json(project_root: Path, issue_number: Optional[int] = None) -> Tuple[Optional[List], Path]`: discover and combine architecture files via `find_architecture_for_project`
- `_parse_llm_response(response: str) -> Tuple[List[str], bool, List[Dict]]`: extract modules (deduplicated), deps validity, corrections
- `_apply_architecture_corrections(project_root: Path, corrections: List[Dict], architecture: Optional[List[Dict]] = None, quiet: bool = False) -> List[Dict]`: for each correction, locate the *owning* architecture.json by `filename`, mutate that entry's dependencies, and write back preserving the original on-disk shape (bare-list or `{prd_files, modules}` dict). Skip corrections with 0 owners (warn: filename not found) or >1 owners (warn: ambiguous duplicate, refusing to write). When `architecture` is supplied, also mutates the combined in-memory list so the downstream graph build sees the new deps. Never writes the combined list to a single primary path — that would flatten nested arches into root (issue #1060).
- `_is_catchall_match(basename: str, config: Dict) -> bool`: check if match is only from wildcard patterns (specificity 0)
- `_is_broad_basename_glob_match(basename: str, config: Dict, context_name: str) -> bool`: check if a nested context matched only via broad basename globs such as `*llm*`
- `_prompt_exists_for_context(candidate_dir: Path, config: Dict, context_name: str, basename: str) -> bool`: verify a nested broad-glob match has an exact `<basename>_*.prompt` in that context's prompts_dir
- `_resolve_module_cwd(basename: str, project_root: Path) -> Path`: determine correct working directory for a module using nearest-config-wins .pddrc resolution (nested .pddrc takes precedence over root)
- `_run_single_dry_run(basename: str, cwd: Path, quiet: bool) -> Tuple[bool, str]`: run pdd sync dry-run
- `_llm_fix_dry_run_failure(basename, project_root, dry_run_error, ...) -> Tuple[bool, Optional[Path], float, str]`: LLM fallback for failed dry-runs
- `_prompt_contract_errors_for_module(basename: str, cwd: Path, project_root: Path) -> List[str]`: deterministic prompt-contract preflight for the matching architecture module
- `_prompt_contract_strict_self_context_required(prompt_path: Path, project_root: Path) -> bool`: return True when the prompt file is changed according to git status or diff against main, enabling staged strict no-self-include checks
- `_format_prompt_contract_preflight_error(basename: str, errors: List[str]) -> str`: format dry-run validation errors for prompt-contract failures
- `_run_dry_run_validation(modules, project_root, ...) -> Tuple[bool, Dict[str, Path], List[str], float]`: orchestrate dry-run validation
- `_filter_already_synced(modules, module_cwds, quiet) -> List[str]`: filter out fully-synced modules via fingerprint check
- `_print_global_sync_plan(analysis: GlobalSyncAnalysis, ordered_modules: List[str], warnings: List[str], budget: Optional[float] = None, *, verbose: bool = False) -> None`: render the dry-run plan. Default behaviour prints architecture-graph `warnings` individually (these are actionable cross-arch dep warnings) and replaces the per-module loop over `analysis.skipped_modules` with a single compact `Out of Tier 1 scope: ...` roll-up bucketed by operation (`example`, `test`, `verify`, `update`, `fix`, `crash`, `no-prompt fixture`, `other`). When `verbose=True`, also emit one `[yellow]Warning: <entry>[/yellow]` line per skipped module after the roll-up so operators can restore the legacy enumeration for debugging. The new keyword-only `verbose` parameter is additive and defaults to `False` for backward compatibility.
- `run_global_sync(...) -> Tuple[bool, str, float, str]`: no-argument Tier 1 global sync over architecture modules. Forwards its `verbose` flag to `_print_global_sync_plan(..., verbose=verbose)` so the top-level `pdd --verbose` opt-in restores per-module skipped-module detail.
- `_post_error_comment(owner, repo, issue_number, message)`: post error to GitHub issue

% Dependencies

% Error Handling
- Return `(False, "gh CLI not found...", 0.0, "")` if `gh` command not available
- Return `(False, "Invalid GitHub issue URL: {url}", 0.0, "")` if URL parsing fails
- Return `(False, "Failed to fetch issue: {error}", 0.0, "")` if GitHub API fails
- Return `(False, "LLM identified no modules to sync", cost, provider)` if LLM returns empty list and the branch diff is not verifiably runtime-template-only
- Return `(False, "No valid modules to sync (all basenames were invalid: ...)", cost, provider)` if all basenames fail validation
- Return `(False, "Dry-run validation failed:\n{details}", cost, provider)` if dry-run validation fails
- Return `(True, "Dry run complete: N module(s) would sync: ...", cost, provider)` when `dry_run=True` and at least one module remains after fingerprint filtering
- Return `(True, "All modules are already synced — nothing to do.", cost, provider)` if fingerprint check filters all modules
- Return `(True, "All modules are already synced — nothing to do.", cost, provider)` if the runtime-LLM-template filter (Req 9, Req 10, or dry-run step 0) removes every selected basename. Issues that touch only runtime `*_LLM.prompt` templates are a successful no-op because those templates are already consumed directly by their owning code modules and there is nothing to regenerate. Do not invoke `pdd sync` or the LLM dry-run fallback in this case.
- Post error comment on GitHub issue when LLM fails, basenames invalid, or dry-run fails (if `use_github_state` is True)

% Deliverables
- Code: `pdd/agentic_sync.py`


===== agentic_sync_runner_python.prompt =====
<include>context/python_preamble.prompt</include>
<pdd-dependency>architecture_sync_python.prompt</pdd-dependency>
<pdd-dependency>agentic_langtest_python.prompt</pdd-dependency>
<pdd-dependency>agentic_test_orchestrator_python.prompt</pdd-dependency>
<pdd-dependency>sync_order_python.prompt</pdd-dependency>

% Goal
Write the `pdd/agentic_sync_runner.py` module.

% Role & Scope
Parallel sync engine that runs `pdd sync` for multiple modules concurrently using a ThreadPoolExecutor, respecting dependency ordering. Posts live progress updates to a GitHub issue comment. Supports state persistence for resumability across runs, phase tracking, and graceful interrupt handling.

% Requirements
1. Class: `AsyncSyncRunner(basenames, dep_graph, sync_options, github_info, quiet, verbose, issue_url, module_cwds, module_targets, initial_cost=0.0)`
   - `basenames: List[str]` — modules to sync
   - `dep_graph: Dict[str, List[str]]` — basename -> [dependency basenames]
   - `sync_options: Dict` — budget, total_budget, target_coverage, skip_verify, skip_tests, agentic, no_steer, max_attempts, one_session, local, timeout_adder, context, strength, temperature
   - `github_info: Optional[Dict]` — owner, repo, issue_number, cwd (None to disable comments)
   - `issue_url: Optional[str]` — GitHub issue URL for state persistence (None disables resumability)
   - `module_cwds: Optional[Dict[str, Path]]` — per-module working directories (defaults to project_root)
   - `module_targets: Optional[Dict[str, str]]` — optional display-key -> actual sync basename map. This allows callers such as global sync to schedule scoped keys like `examples/prompts_linter:report` while executing `pdd sync report` in that module's cwd.
   - `initial_cost: float` — pre-runner cost (LLM module identification and dry-run fallback) to include in total cost display and return value (default 0.0)
   - Tracks per-module state: pending -> running -> success | failed
2. Method: `run() -> Tuple[bool, str, float]` — returns (all_success, summary_message, total_cost) where total_cost includes initial_cost + per-module costs
3. Use `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` with `MAX_WORKERS = 4`; when `sync_options["total_budget"]` is set, run sequentially and pass only the remaining total budget to each child process so the total budget is not multiplied per module.
4. Dependency-aware scheduling, SCC-aware so legitimate dependency cycles (Python late-imports, etc.) do not deadlock the runner:
   - At construction, compute strongly connected components of the basename-induced subgraph of `dep_graph` via `pdd.sync_order.compute_sccs`. Build:
     - `self._scc_of: Dict[str, frozenset[str]]` mapping every in-target basename to its SCC (frozenset of co-cyclic peers).
     - `self._cyclic_sccs: Set[frozenset[str]]` containing every SCC that participates in a real cycle (size > 1, or a 1-node SCC with a self-loop). A trivial SCC (single node, no self-loop) is NOT in this set, so soft-edge logic only applies to actual cycle members.
   - For a non-cycle pending module, it is ready only when every cross-target dep has status "success" — for any in-target dep, EVERY member of the dep's SCC must be "success" (the consumer waits for the whole upstream SCC, not just the named dep). External (not in `basenames`) deps are assumed already synced.
   - For a pending cycle member, the SCC's effective dependencies are the union of every member's cross-SCC deps; intra-SCC edges (including self-loops) are soft. Soft-edge means a pending or success peer does not block readiness — only a failed peer does. The same whole-upstream-SCC rule applies to every cross-SCC dep of every cycle member, so an SCC may not start while ANY peer's external dep is still pending (or failed).
   - Execution within a cyclic SCC is serialized: at most one member of a cyclic SCC may be running or scheduled per `_get_ready_modules` pass.
   - Blocked-module classification operates on the SCC condensation (a DAG): a pending module is blocked if its SCC has any failed member, any cross-SCC dep is failed, or any cross-SCC dep's SCC is itself blocked. Walking the condensation rather than the raw dep_graph avoids the per-module DFS caching incorrect False on cycle re-entry.
5. Dependencies outside `basenames` are omitted from `dep_graph` (partial sync). Callers should rely on `build_dep_graph_from_architecture` warnings for visibility when an architecture edge points outside the target set; the runner still assumes those deps are out of scope for this run.
6. Failure scheduling: when a module fails, set `self.failed = True`, but do not globally pause the queue. Failed modules block only modules that depend on them through `dep_graph`; unrelated modules whose own dependencies are satisfied must continue to run so partial progress is preserved and future retries can resume from more completed modules. Blocked-module classification must be transitive: if `a` fails and `b` depends on `a`, then pending modules that depend on `b` are also blocked. Only total-budget exhaustion stops new scheduling globally, and `_budget_exhausted()` must be checked after every completed future, including failed module results, before scheduling more work.
7. Each module runs as a `pdd sync` subprocess with `--force`, `--agentic`, `--no-steer` flags; append global `--local` before `sync` if `sync_options["local"]` is truthy; append `--target-coverage` if `sync_options["target_coverage"]` is not None; append `--one-session` if `sync_options["one_session"]` is truthy
8. Use `PDD_OUTPUT_COST_PATH` env var for cost capture from subprocess (CSV format)
9. Sync failure detection: exit code != 0 OR "Overall status:" line contains "Failed"
9a. Failed-module error-summary contract (issue #1399): the `error` string returned for a failed module MUST start with a deterministic failure reason — the `Overall status: Failed` line if present in stdout, otherwise `f"Exit code {exit_code}"`. Then append high-signal lines from stdout+stderr (those containing `error`/`failed`/`traceback`/`exception`/`abort`, lowercased), filtering out `INFO`/`DEBUG` log lines and known nonfatal warnings (module-level constant `_NONFATAL_WARNING_SUBSTRINGS`, currently `("ContentSelector failed",)`, exposed via helper `_is_nonfatal_warning(line)`). Drop any candidate line equal to the failure reason so it is not echoed twice. If no high-signal line exists, fall back to labeled `--- stderr tail ---` and `--- stdout tail ---` blocks (last 10 non-blank, non-warning, non-failure-reason lines each). Cap keyword-line summary at last 20 lines. Console preview: `error[:500]`.
9b. Architecture-conformance / public-surface / test-churn retry/repair (issues #865, #1012): when a per-module `pdd sync` subprocess fails AND the captured stdout/stderr contains a repairable generation failure — detected via any of the line prefixes `Architecture conformance error for `, `Public surface regression for `, or `Test churn threshold exceeded for ` — the runner MUST treat it as a repairable generation failure and retry the same module up to `MAX_CONFORMANCE_ATTEMPTS = 3` total attempts (initial attempt + up to 2 repair attempts). Each repair attempt MUST set the `PDD_REPAIR_DIRECTIVE` environment variable in the subprocess env to a directive built by the appropriate parser — `_parse_conformance_failure(stdout, stderr)`, `_parse_public_surface_failure(stdout, stderr)`, or `_parse_test_churn_failure(stdout, stderr)` — that names the exact offending symbols / churn ratio and forbids editing `architecture.json` or rewriting unrelated tests. The directive MUST NOT be set on the first attempt. Retries MUST NOT advance the per-module `current_phase` to a later phase; these failures restart the same generate-then-verify cycle. Repair retries do NOT count against any per-module test/fix attempt budget — they are a separate, prompt-level repair loop. When `total_budget` is set, every retry command MUST receive the remaining total budget after subtracting both persisted module costs and the current module's in-flight retry costs; if the in-flight repair cost exhausts the remaining total budget, stop retrying before launching another child process and record a hard failure. The runner MUST also forward the parent's resolved `.pddrc` context and `--strength` (when present in `sync_options`) on EVERY retry attempt so a flaky regeneration cannot silently switch model or context between attempts.
9c. Short-circuit on stuck failure signature: `_parse_conformance_failure`, `_parse_public_surface_failure`, and `_parse_test_churn_failure` MUST each return a normalized, sortable failure-signature tuple — sorted missing symbols for conformance, sorted removed symbols for public-surface, and `(round(churn_ratio, 2), pre_line_count)` for test-churn. If the failure signature on attempt N is identical to attempt N-1 (the LLM produced the same omission/regression/rewrite twice in a row), the runner MUST stop retrying immediately and record the module as failed — additional retries with identical input are wasted budget. The recorded `error` MUST include the structured failure block (conformance, public-surface, or test-churn) so callers (including the GitHub App) can surface it.
9d. Hard-failure diagnostic: when a module fails after all repair retries are exhausted (or short-circuits per 9c), the recorded `error` MUST include, in order: (1) the original failure reason from 9a, (2) a structured block whose header depends on the failure class — `=== architecture conformance failure ===` listing `prompt`, `output`, `expected`, `found`, `missing` (built by `build_conformance_hard_failure_from_error`); `=== public surface regression ===` listing `prompt`, `output`, `removed`, `pre_surface_size`, `post_surface_size`, plus the `BREAKING-CHANGE:` opt-in note (built by `build_public_surface_hard_failure_from_error`); or `=== test churn threshold exceeded ===` listing `prompt`, `output`, `ratio`, `threshold`, `pre_line_count`, `post_line_count`, plus the `BREAKING-CHANGE:` opt-in note (built by `build_test_churn_hard_failure_from_error`) — one line each, (3) a local repro line of the form `Reproduce locally: pdd sync <basename>`, and (4) an `--- env ---` fingerprint block emitted via helper `_env_fingerprint()` containing `pdd.__file__`, `pdd --version` output, current git SHA, working-tree dirty status (`clean` or `dirty`), and whether the active `pdd` resolves inside the checked-out repo or `site-packages`. The fingerprint helper MUST be best-effort (each probe wrapped in try/except) so a missing git binary or an importable-but-uninstalled `pdd` never raises out of the failure path. All three `build_*_hard_failure_from_error` helpers MUST be importable from `pdd.agentic_sync_runner` so `sync_main` and `sync_orchestration` can reuse them verbatim.
10. Per-module timeout: `MODULE_TIMEOUT = int(os.environ.get('PDD_MODULE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS', '2700'))` seconds (default 45 minutes); effective cap per module is `MODULE_TIMEOUT + max(0.0, float(sync_options.get('timeout_adder') or 0.0))` so `--timeout-adder` extends without ever shrinking it. Timeout-error message reports the effective seconds.
11. Child command construction must forward resolved global model/config knobs before the `sync` subcommand: `--context`, `--strength`, and `--temperature` when present in `sync_options`, plus `--local` when requested. This keeps repair retries pinned to the same .pddrc context/model settings instead of letting each child re-resolve them differently.
11. State persistence: save state to `.pdd/agentic_sync_state.json` after each module completion; restore on init if issue_url matches
12. Resumability: skip modules with status "success" in saved state; allow partial resumption when module lists differ
13. Delete state file on full success; preserve on failure
14. Signal handling: install SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers that kill child process groups before re-raising
15. Phase tracking: parse `PDD_PHASE: <phase>` markers from subprocess stdout; update `ModuleState.current_phase` and `completed_phases`
16. Skip phases (prefixed `skip:`) are not added to completed_phases
17. Rate-limit GitHub comment updates for phase changes to once per 15 seconds
18. Per-module working directories: use `module_cwds[basename]` if present, else `project_root`. When building the child command, append `module_targets.get(basename, basename)` as the actual `pdd sync` target so display keys can differ from executable basenames.
19. Subprocess env: include `PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1` for real-time output
20. Forward "Successfully submitted example" messages from child stdout to parent console
21. Heartbeat logging: during long-running syncs, print progress updates every 60s. Prefer parsed `PDD_PHASE` state — `f" — phase: {current_phase} ({len(completed_phases)} done)"` — so operators see real progress through the generate/test/fix phases instead of a stale `Preprocessing complete` line. Fall back to the last non-box-drawing stdout line only when no phase has been reported yet.

% Dataclass: `ModuleState`
- `status: str` — "pending", "running", "success", "failed"
- `start_time: Optional[float]`
- `end_time: Optional[float]`
- `cost: float`
- `error: Optional[str]`
- `current_phase: Optional[str]` — current phase name or None
- `completed_phases: List[str]` — list of completed phase names (excludes skip: phases)

% GitHub Comment Updates
- Create comment on first update (`gh api POST`), store `comment_id`
- Before creating a new comment, search existing issue comments with `gh api .../issues/{issue_number}/comments --paginate --slurp` for the newest body beginning with `## PDD Agentic Sync Progress\nIssue: #{issue_number}`. Reuse that comment id with PATCH so resumed durable runs and fresh worker reruns do not spam duplicate progress comments.
- Subsequent updates: PATCH existing comment
- If PATCH fails, clear `comment_id` and continue without failing sync; GitHub comment failures must never break the sync run.
- Markdown table format: Module | Status | Phase | Duration | Cost
- Phase column: running modules show `current_phase (N done)`, completed modules show `N phases` or `-`
- Status icons: pending (hourglass), running (sync), success (checkmark), failed (cross)
- Footer: total cost (initial_cost + sum of per-module costs), overall status (in progress / continuing independent modules after failure / paused / all success)
- Failed-module details: include structured error details in `<details>` blocks, but enforce a total GitHub comment body cap below GitHub's 65,536-character limit before every POST/PATCH. Use a module-level cap constant (for example 60,000 chars), dynamically shrink per-module failure excerpts as the number of failures grows, append a truncation/omission summary, and apply a final safety truncation immediately before calling `gh api` so GitHub App progress comments cannot be silently rejected for oversized bodies.

% NamedTuple: `DepGraphFromArchitectureResult`
- `graph: Dict[str, List[str]]` — basename -> list of dependency basenames **within the target set only** (scheduled subgraph)
- `warnings: List[str]` — human-readable issues that did **not** prevent building the subgraph; operators use these to see silent omissions

% Function: `build_dep_graph_from_architecture(arch_path: Path, target_basenames: List[str]) -> DepGraphFromArchitectureResult`
- Load architecture.json and match target basenames against architecture entries using both prompt `filename` aliases and code `filepath` aliases. This is required for branch-diff targets such as `src/config`, where `filename` may be only `config_Python.prompt` but `filepath` is `src/config.py`.
- Preserve the original `target_basenames` strings as graph keys/dependencies; do not normalize `src/config` to `config` before scheduling, because those path-qualified strings are needed for .pddrc context routing.
- Build subgraph: only include edges where **both** endpoints resolve to basenames in `target_basenames` (partial sync: edges to modules outside the target set are **omitted** from `graph`, not added as fake nodes)
- Exclude self-dependencies
- Return empty deps for basenames not found in architecture.json
- **Warnings (append to `warnings`, do not raise):**
  - **Orphan dependency:** a module lists a dependency filename that has **no** matching architecture entry (cannot resolve basename)
  - **Partial-sync edge:** a dependency resolves to a basename **not** in `target_basenames` — the edge is still omitted from `graph` (assumption: that module is already synced or out of scope), but the warning records that the schedule is intentionally minimal, not a full view of architecture.json

% Function: `build_dep_graph_from_architecture_data(architecture: Any, target_basenames: List[str], *, source_name: str = "architecture.json") -> DepGraphFromArchitectureResult`
- Build the same scheduled subgraph from already-loaded architecture data.
- Use this for combined architecture data that merges root and nested architecture.json files.

% Helper: `_basename_from_architecture_filename(filename: str) -> Optional[str]`
- Convert an architecture `filename` to a sync basename while preserving subdirectory prefixes.
- Examples: `agentic_sync_python.prompt` -> `agentic_sync`; `commands/maintenance_python.prompt` -> `commands/maintenance`; `_LLM.prompt` entries return `None`.
- Strip only final suffixes that are known languages according to PDD's language catalog (`construct_paths._is_known_language`), not a hard-coded regex list. This must handle compound frontend languages such as `TypeScriptReact` and `JavaScriptReact` (`tasks_page_TypeScriptReact.prompt` -> `tasks_page`) and future catalog additions.

% Helper: `_basename_from_architecture_filepath(filepath: str) -> Optional[str]`
- Convert an architecture `filepath` to a sync basename by removing the final file extension and preserving directories.
- Examples: `src/config.py` -> `src/config`; `src/clients/firestore_client.py` -> `src/clients/firestore_client`.

% Helper: `_architecture_entry_aliases(entry: Dict[str, Any]) -> set[str]`
- Return every basename that can identify an architecture entry, including `_basename_from_architecture_filename(entry["filename"])` and `_basename_from_architecture_filepath(entry["filepath"])` when present.

% Helper: `_target_basename_aliases(target_basename: str) -> set[str]`
- Return aliases for a requested sync target, including the original target and the language-suffix-stripped variant while preserving directories (for example `src/config_Python` -> `src/config`).
- Use the same catalog-aware language suffix stripping as `_basename_from_architecture_filename`; target aliases and architecture filename aliases must not diverge.

% Helper Functions
- `_find_pdd_executable() -> Optional[str]`: find pdd binary (same pattern as `server/jobs.py`)
- `_parse_cost_from_csv(csv_path: str) -> float`: sum cost column from PDD_OUTPUT_COST_PATH CSV
- `_format_duration(start, end) -> str`: format seconds as "Xs" or "Xm Ys"
- `_parse_conformance_failure(stdout: str, stderr: str) -> Optional[Tuple[str, Tuple[str, ...]]]`: scan combined stdout+stderr for the line prefix `Architecture conformance error for ` and, when matched, return `(repair_directive, missing_symbols)` where `missing_symbols` is a sorted tuple of the symbols listed after any of the following inline shapes (route each into its own directive bucket — they MUST NOT be merged):
    - (a) `declared symbols missing from generated code:` — default `ArchitectureConformanceError` shape (architecture.json symbol-existence check).
    - (b) `Python code uses camelCase names (...)` parenthesised list — camelCase guard.
    - (c) `declares function(s)/method(s) missing from the generated code:` — pdd-interface missing-function/method shape emitted by `code_generator_main._verify_pdd_interface_signatures` (e.g. bare dotted names like `ContentSelector.select`).
    - (d) `declares parameter(s) missing from the generated code:` — pdd-interface missing-parameter shape (dotted entries like `update_main.sync_metadata`).
    - (e) `declares parameter(s) whose signature drifted in the generated code:` — pdd-interface drift shape (dotted symbol followed by a parenthesised diagnostic like `(annotation: declared \`bool\`, found \`str\`)` or `(default: declared \`False\`, found \`<no default>\`)`).
  All five shapes MUST route into the same repair-retry loop. The parser MUST stop the missing-symbol capture before `Output:`, `Expected:`, `Found:`, or the next `the prompt's <pdd-interface>` sentence so dotted symbols such as `Class.method` are preserved while diagnostics are not treated as symbols. For shape (e), the per-entry split MUST be parenthesis-aware (track depth and split on top-level commas) so `(annotation: declared \`bool\`, found \`str\`)` survives intact; strip the parenthesised diagnostic when contributing to `missing_symbols` so the stuck-symbol-set short-circuit compares canonical dotted symbols.
  The `repair_directive` MUST be tailored to which shape produced each symbol: legacy export-missing entries (shapes (a) and (b)) go under `Required missing exports:` with the `Add these exact exports. Do not modify architecture.json. Do not remove existing valid exports.` footer; pdd-interface entries go under a separate section emitting `- Add the following missing function(s)/method(s) declared in the prompt: \`<name>[, ...]\`.` for shape (c), `- On \`<fully-qualified-function>\`, add the following missing parameter(s) to the signature and corresponding code paths: \`<param>[, ...]\`.` for shape (d) (regrouped via `rpartition('.')` so `ContentSelector.select.mode` attaches to `ContentSelector.select`, not `ContentSelector`), and `- Update the generated code so parameter <func.param> (<kind>: declared \`<src>\`, found \`<src>\`) matches the prompt.` for shape (e). The pdd-interface section MUST end with the line `Do not remove the declared parameters from the prompt's <pdd-interface>. The prompt is the source of truth — update the generated code to match it.` Return `None` when no conformance failure or no concrete missing/offending symbol is detected so callers fall through to the normal error path instead of retrying with an empty directive.
- `_env_fingerprint() -> str`: return a multi-line `--- env ---` block describing the active PDD installation (best-effort, never raises). Include: `pdd.__file__` (or `<unimportable>`), `pdd --version` stdout (first line; `<unavailable>` on error), current git SHA from `git rev-parse HEAD` (or `<no-git>`), `git status --porcelain` -> `clean` or `dirty` (or `<unknown>`), and a `source: repo|site-packages|unknown` line derived from whether `pdd.__file__` lives under the current working directory tree.
- `_parse_public_surface_failure(stdout: str, stderr: str) -> Optional[Tuple[str, Tuple[str, ...]]]` (#1012): scan combined stdout+stderr for the line prefix `Public surface regression for ` and, when matched, return `(repair_directive, signature)` where `signature` preserves distinct `removed:<symbol>` and `signature_changed:<symbol>` entries from the structured `removed:` and `signature_changed:` lines. The `repair_directive` MUST instruct the LLM to restore missing public symbols and compatible signatures in separate sections. Return `None` when no public-surface failure is detected so callers fall through to the normal error path.
- `_parse_test_churn_failure(stdout: str, stderr: str) -> Optional[Tuple[str, Tuple[float, int]]]` (#1012): scan combined stdout+stderr for the line prefix `Test churn threshold exceeded for ` and, when matched, return `(repair_directive, signature)` where `signature` is `(round(ratio, 2), pre_line_count)` extracted from the `ratio:` and `pre_line_count:` lines. The `repair_directive` MUST instruct the LLM to extend the existing test file rather than rewrite it, preserving existing test function names and unrelated coverage, and MUST include the footer `Add new tests for the prompt change without deleting accumulated regression tests. If a wholesale rewrite is intentional, add a line beginning with \`BREAKING-CHANGE:\` to the prompt body.` Return `None` when no test-churn failure is detected.
- `build_public_surface_hard_failure_from_error(exc: PublicSurfaceRegressionError, basename: str) -> str` (#1012): render the `=== public surface regression ===` block described in 9d, ending with `Reproduce locally: pdd sync <basename>` and the `_env_fingerprint()` output. Importable from `pdd.agentic_sync_runner` so `sync_main` and `sync_orchestration` can reuse it verbatim.
- `build_test_churn_hard_failure_from_error(exc: TestChurnError, basename: str) -> str` (#1012): render the `=== test churn threshold exceeded ===` block described in 9d, ending with `Reproduce locally: pdd sync <basename>` and the `_env_fingerprint()` output. Importable from `pdd.agentic_sync_runner`.

% State Management (Private Methods)
- `_state_file_path() -> Path`: return `.pdd/agentic_sync_state.json` path
- `_load_state()`: restore succeeded modules from state file if issue_url matches
- `_save_state()`: atomically persist state (temp file + os.replace)
- `_delete_state()`: remove state file (called on full success)

% Signal Handling
- Install SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers in `run()` that call `_kill_children()` then restore and re-raise
- Track child process groups in `self._child_pgids`
- `_kill_children()`: SIGTERM all tracked pgids, wait 1s, then SIGKILL survivors

% Phase Tracking
- `_on_phase_change(basename, phase)`: move current_phase to completed_phases, set new current_phase
- Call `_update_github_comment_throttled()` after phase change
- `_update_github_comment_throttled()`: update at most once per 15 seconds

% Subprocess Execution
- Use `subprocess.Popen` with `start_new_session=True` to create process group
- Use threaded pipe readers to prevent deadlock when output exceeds OS buffer
- Parse `PDD_PHASE:` markers from stdout lines in reader thread
- Heartbeat: poll with timeout every 60s; print last informative output line (skip box-drawing chars)
- Box-drawing filter: `_BOX_CHARS_RE` matches lines with only `╠╗║╔═║╔╗║║╠╣╦╩╦-|+` and whitespace

% Dependencies
<pdd.agentic_change><include>context/agentic_change_example.py</include></pdd.agentic_change>
<pdd.sync_order><include>context/sync_order_example.py</include></pdd.sync_order>
<pdd.agentic_common><include select="def:example_github_state_helpers,def:example_post_step_comment,def:example_run_agentic_task">context/agentic_common_example.py</include></pdd.agentic_common>
<pdd.architecture_sync>
    <include select="def:example_validate_dependencies,def:example_get_entry">context/architecture_sync_example.py</include>
</pdd.architecture_sync>
<pdd.agentic_langtest>
    <include select="def:default_verify_cmd_for">context/agentic_langtest_example.py</include>
</pdd.agentic_langtest>
<pdd.agentic_test_orchestrator>
    <include select="def:example_state_resumption">context/agentic_test_orchestrator_example.py</include>
</pdd.agentic_test_orchestrator>

% Instructions
- Use `threading.Lock` for thread-safe state updates
- Reuse `_run_gh_command` from `agentic_change.py` for GitHub comment API calls
- Set subprocess env: `CI=1`, `PDD_FORCE=1`, `TERM=dumb`, `PDD_AUTO_UPDATE=false`, `PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1` for headless execution
- Fallback to `sys.executable -m pdd` if `_find_pdd_executable()` returns None
- Separate `run()` into `run()` (signal handler setup) and `_run_inner()` (actual execution loop)
- `_record_result()`: finalize current_phase by moving to completed_phases (unless skip: prefixed), then persist state

===== agentic_test_generate_LLM.prompt =====
You are generating unit tests for a PDD (Prompt-Driven Development) project.

## PDD Principle
The PROMPT FILE is the source of truth. Tests verify that code conforms to the specification.

## Files (you have full read/write access)
- Prompt file (THE SPEC): {prompt_path}
- Code file to test: {code_path}
- Output test file: {test_path}
- Project root: {project_root}

## Specification
<prompt_content>
{prompt_content}
</prompt_content>

## Code Under Test
<code_under_test>
{code_content}
</code_under_test>

## Your Task

1. **Analyze the Project**
   - Explore project structure to identify the language and existing test framework
   - Look for: package.json, jest.config.*, tsconfig.json, pom.xml, build.gradle, Cargo.toml, go.mod, etc.
   - Check existing tests to understand patterns and conventions
   - Determine the file extension for tests (e.g., .test.ts, .spec.js, Test.java, _test.go)

2. **Set Up Test Environment**
   - Install missing dependencies if needed (npm install, mvn install, cargo build, etc.)
   - Ensure test framework is available (Jest, JUnit, Go testing, Rust testing, etc.)
   - Create test configuration files if needed and none exist

3. **Generate Tests**
   - Create the test file at the specified output path with the CORRECT extension for the language
   - Follow the project's existing test patterns and conventions
   - Write comprehensive tests covering:
     - Normal functionality as described in the prompt
     - Edge cases
     - Error handling
   - Use appropriate assertions for the test framework
   - MOCKING RULE: Mock at external boundaries, test internal logic directly.
     - ALWAYS mock: HTTP clients, database connections, auth providers, third-party SDKs,
       message queues, file storage, external service SDKs (firebase-admin, stripe, boto3, etc.)
     - NEVER mock: The function under test, pure computation, data validation, business logic
     - Mock return values MUST match the real return type from the source code — do not invent response shapes
   - If the code depends on environment variables for API keys/credentials:
     - Mock the external service boundary, not just the env var
     - Use clearly fake values (e.g., "test-api-key-not-real") so tests never hit real services
   - The goal: test as much of YOUR code as possible while isolating external services that need credentials

4. **Run Tests**
   - Execute the tests using the appropriate test runner discovered in step 1
   - If tests fail, analyze the error and fix the tests
   - Repeat until all tests pass

5. **Output JSON**
   Output a JSON object with these fields:
   - success: bool (true if tests pass)
   - message: str (summary of what was done)
   - test_file: str (path to generated test file)
   - changed_files: list[str] (all files created/modified)

   IMPORTANT: The JSON output MUST be your very last text output.
   Do NOT call any tools or output any additional text after the JSON block.

## Critical Rules
- The prompt file defines expected behavior - tests should verify this
- DO NOT assume any specific test framework - discover it from the project
- If no test framework exists, suggest and install an appropriate one for the language
- Match existing test style and conventions in the project
- Use the CORRECT file extension for the test file based on the language
- IMPORTANT: Read actual source files before assuming what functions/classes exist
- You MUST run the generated test file and ensure it passes before completing

## Python-Specific Rules
When the language is Python:
- Use `pytest` as the test framework
- CRITICAL IMPORT RULE: Import the module by its filename stem, NOT via directory path.
  - CORRECT: `from hello import hello` (with sys.path setup)
  - WRONG:  `from src.hello import hello`
- Add sys.path setup at the top of the test file so pytest can find the code:
  ```python
  import sys, os
  sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), '..', 'src'))
  ```
- Target 90%+ code coverage
- Use pytest fixtures, parametrize, and mock patterns



===== agentic_test_generate_python.prompt =====
<include>context/python_preamble.prompt</include>

% Goal
Write the pdd/agentic_test_generate.py module.

% Role & Scope
Agentic test generation for non-Python languages. Instead of using a single LLM
call (which often produces incorrect test file extensions or doesn't follow the
correct framework), this module delegates to an agentic CLI that can:
1. Explore the project structure
2. Discover test frameworks (Jest, JUnit, Go test, Cargo test, etc.)
3. Generate tests
4. Run them to verify they pass

% Entry Point
`run_agentic_test_generate(prompt_file, code_file, output_test_file, *, verbose: bool = False, quiet: bool = False, repair_directive: str | None = None)`

Returns: `tuple[str, float, str, bool, str]` → `(generated_content, cost, model_name, success, error_message)`
- generated_content: The content of the generated test file
- cost: Estimated LLM cost
- model_name: Model/provider used (formatted as "agentic-{provider}")
- success: Whether the agentic test generation succeeded (tests passed)
- error_message: Empty string on success; on failure, the error/message from run_agentic_task

% LLM Prompt Template
Uses `agentic_test_generate_LLM` (loaded via load_prompt_template).

Template placeholders:
- `{prompt_path}`: Absolute path to prompt file
- `{code_path}`: Absolute path to code file
- `{test_path}`: Absolute path to output test file
- `{project_root}`: Absolute path to project root
- `{prompt_content}`: Content of prompt file
- `{code_content}`: Content of code file

% Requirements
1. Single-pass agentic execution (no retry loops)
2. Record file mtimes before agent runs, detect changes after
3. Agent writes the test file directly; module reads it back for return value
4. If expected output file doesn't exist, check changed_files for any new test/spec files. When the agent wrote to such an alternate path (e.g. `__tests__/foo.test.ts` instead of the canonical `output_test_file`), the OUTER churn check in `cmd_test_main` compares the alt-path's NEW content against the CANONICAL path's pre-existing content (empty when canonical didn't exist), letting a 20-test alt file shrink to 1 without tripping `TestChurnError`. To close that gap, BEFORE running the agent take a working-tree content snapshot of every existing file matching `_is_test_output_path` under `project_root` via `_snapshot_pre_test_contents(project_root, mtimes_before.keys()) -> dict[Path, str]`; this captures tracked-clean, tracked-dirty, AND untracked files alike (so neither an untracked alt-path test nor uncommitted local edits get treated as first-time generation, and a restore-on-failure never silently discards dirty work). Bound per-file at ~1 MiB to keep snapshot memory predictable; files above the cap fall through to first-time-generation behavior. Lazy-import `_is_test_output_path` from `code_generator_main` to avoid a circular dependency. AFTER the agent runs, when the alt-path fallback fires, look up the alt path's prior content from this snapshot (NOT from `git show HEAD:<rel>` — that would miss untracked alt files entirely and on restore would clobber the user's local dirty edits with HEAD) and call `_verify_test_churn(existing_code=prior, generated_code=alt_content, prompt_name=prompt_file.name, output_path=str(alt_path), prompt_content=prompt_content)`. On `TestChurnError`: set `total_cost`/`model_name` on the exception, RESTORE the alt path's snapshotted pre-run content to disk (so the repair loop sees the actual pre-sync working-tree state), then re-raise. Lazy-import `_verify_test_churn`/`TestChurnError` from `code_generator_main`.
5. Parse JSON from agent output to get success/message metadata
6. Return empty string for content if no test file was generated
7. **Repair-directive injection via explicit kwarg** (#1012, F-A / F-H): `run_agentic_test_generate` accepts a `repair_directive: str | None = None` keyword-only parameter. After reading `prompt_content` from the prompt file on disk and BEFORE passing it into the agent instruction template format, if `repair_directive` is non-empty (after stripping) append a `<test_repair_directive>...</test_repair_directive>` block to `prompt_content` containing the stripped directive. This mirrors the `<architecture_repair_directive>` injection in `code_generator_main` and the native-path injection in `cmd_test_main`, so test-op retries from `sync_orchestration._run_test_op_with_churn_retry` actually see a different prompt on the second attempt. **`run_agentic_test_generate` MUST NOT read `PDD_REPAIR_DIRECTIVE` from `os.environ`**: a stale outer value would otherwise contaminate direct invocations that have no active retry context. The retry helpers source the directive from their loop-local state and pass it explicitly through `cmd_test_main(..., repair_directive=...)` which forwards it here. Do NOT mutate the on-disk prompt file — only the in-process `prompt_content` flowing into `template.format(...)` is augmented.

% Helper Functions

### `_get_file_mtimes(root: Path) -> dict[Path, float]`
Recursively scan directory to record file modification times.
Ignores: .git, __pycache__, .venv, venv, node_modules, .idea, .vscode, .pdd

### `_extract_json_from_text(text: str) -> dict[str, Any] | None`
Extract JSON object from text (handles markdown code blocks and raw JSON).

### `_read_generated_test_file(test_file: Path) -> str`
Read the generated test file content if it exists, empty string otherwise.

### `_snapshot_pre_test_contents(project_root: Path, candidate_paths: Iterable[Path]) -> dict[Path, str]`
Read the on-disk content of every file in `candidate_paths` that lives under `project_root` and matches `_is_test_output_path`. Skips files >1 MiB and silently drops paths that fail `OSError`. Used to capture the alt-path churn baseline BEFORE the agent runs, so tracked-clean / tracked-dirty / untracked test files are all covered uniformly. The dict's later `.get(alt_path, "")` lookup and restore-on-failure step give the repair loop the real pre-sync working-tree content instead of HEAD.

### `_detect_changed_files(before, after, project_root) -> list[str]`
Detect which files changed between two mtime snapshots (new, modified, deleted).

% Dependencies
<module_dependencies>
    <pdd.agentic_common><include>context/agentic_common_example.py</include></pdd.agentic_common>
    <pdd.load_prompt_template><include>context/load_prompt_template_example.py</include></pdd.load_prompt_template>
</module_dependencies>

% Deliverables
- Code: `pdd/agentic_test_generate.py`


===== agentic_test_orchestrator_python.prompt =====
<pdd-dependency>agentic_common_python.prompt</pdd-dependency>

% You are an expert LLM Prompt Engineer.
% Role: Senior Backend Engineer / Automation Architect.

# Role and Scope
The module is an **Agentic Test Orchestrator** for an 18-step automated test generation workflow. Its responsibility is to manage the lifecycle of an "issue-to-test" conversion process by executing sequential and conditional agentic tasks. It maintains state, handles workspace isolation via git worktrees, manages complex context passing between steps (including special handling for web/Playwright tests), and supports resume-from-failure functionality using both local and remote (GitHub) state persistence.

# Requirements
1. **Workflow Execution**: Implement a linear sequence of 18 steps (including fractional steps like 5.5) defined by specific prompt templates.
2. **State Management**:
    - Support persistence and resumption using `load_workflow_state` and `save_workflow_state`.
    - Track `total_cost`, `model_used`, and `last_completed_step`.
    - Clear state upon successful completion.
3. **Workspace Isolation**:
    - Automatically create and manage isolated git worktrees for each issue (branch name format: `test/issue-{number}`).
    - When `clean_restart=True`, fetch `+refs/heads/test/issue-{number}:refs/remotes/origin/test/issue-{number}` for Step 17 `--force-with-lease` safety, create the test worktree from the resolved default branch (`origin/main`, `origin/master`, `main`, then `master`) rather than current `HEAD`, and fail clearly if no default branch ref resolves.
    - Handle cleanup of existing worktrees/branches to ensure a clean state.
4. **Context Propagation**:
    - Parse tags from LLM outputs (e.g., `FILES_CREATED`, `TEST_TYPE`, `STOP_CONDITION`).
    - Dynamically detect `likely_ci_cwd` by searching for `.pddrc` files relative to generated test files.
    - Pass outputs from previous steps as context for subsequent steps.
5. **Conditional Logic**:
    - Implement a "Hard Stop" mechanism based on `STOP_CONDITION` tags or specific failure criteria (e.g., duplicate detection).
    - Handle specialized paths for "web" frontend types, including manual testing loops (Steps 6-11) and Playwright integration.
6. **Parallel/Cloud Integration**: Support an optional cloud-based manual testing execution by polling for results in a JSON file if `PDD_CLOUD_RUN` is enabled.
7. **Progress Reporting**: Use `set_agentic_progress` to provide real-time updates on step completion and `rich` for console output.
8. **Resilience**: Implement timeouts per step (mapped in `TEST_STEP_TIMEOUTS`) and utilize a retry mechanism for agentic tasks.
9. **Trusted Step-Comment Flow**: The orchestrator owns visible per-step `## Step N/18:` comments on the GitHub issue, posted via `gh issue comment` with trusted PDD credentials. Step prompts MUST NOT call `gh issue comment` themselves for successful progress — they emit a `<step_report>...</step_report>` block in their final response and the orchestrator extracts and posts it once.
   - Import from `pdd.agentic_common`: `extract_step_report(step_output) -> Optional[str]`, `post_step_comment_once(*, repo_owner, repo_name, issue_number, step_num: int, body: str, posted_steps: Set[int], cwd) -> bool`, and `normalize_step_comments_state(raw) -> Set[int]`.
   - **State shape**: `state["step_comments"]` persists as a sorted `List[int]` on disk; hydrate to `Set[int]` via `normalize_step_comments_state` on resume; persist back as a sorted list before every `save_workflow_state` call (success path, hard-stop early-return, abort).
   - **Composite-key encoding (REQUIRED — helper is `Set[int]`-typed)**: the test workflow has fractional steps (5.5) and an iterated manual-testing loop (steps 8/9/10/11 across `iteration in 1..3`). Project each (step_num, iteration) tuple to a deterministic non-negative int via `iteration * 10000 + int(round(step_num * 10))`. Use `iteration=1` for non-iterative steps; pass the actual loop iteration for the manual-testing-loop steps. This guarantees re-runs across iterations get distinct keys and resume-replay reproduces the same key.
   - **Posting protocol** inside `save_state`: on success and when `description` is provided, compute `report_body = extract_step_report(step_output)`; if `None`, use a short fallback body saying the step completed and raw output is retained in workflow state. Build `f"## Step {step_num}/18: {description}\n\n{report_body}"` and call `post_step_comment_once(...)`. Persist `state["step_comments"] = sorted(step_comments_set)` so the on-disk shape stays canonical. Any exception from extract / post / persist MUST be log-and-continue.
   - **Relationship to legacy `post_step_comment`**: hard-stop / failure comments continue to flow through the legacy helper unchanged. `post_step_comment_once` is strictly additive — it covers the success-comment case the model used to fill (incorrectly) by invoking `gh issue comment` itself.

# Dependencies
<agentic_common>
    <include>context/agentic_common_example.py</include>
</agentic_common>
<pytest_output>
    <include>context/pytest_output_example.py</include>
</pytest_output>
<load_prompt_template>
    <include>context/load_prompt_template_example.py</include>
</load_prompt_template>
<pdd.agentic_langtest>
    <include select="def:default_verify_cmd_for,def:missing_tool_hints">context/agentic_langtest_example.py</include>
</pdd.agentic_langtest>

# Instructions
1. **Timeouts**: Use a mapping for `TEST_STEP_TIMEOUTS` where Step 12 (Generation) and Step 8 (Manual) have the highest limits (1800s and 1000s respectively).
2. **Git Helpers**: Implement private helpers for `_get_git_root`, `_worktree_exists`, `_setup_worktree`, and `_detect_ci_cwd`. The `_detect_ci_cwd` helper must find the project root for sub-projects within a mono-repo.
3. **Parsing Logic**: Use regex to extract file lists from `FILES_CREATED` and `FILES_MODIFIED`. Handle "none" or "n/a" as empty lists.
4. **The Loop (Steps 8-11)**: For web tests, implement a loop (max 3 iterations) that performs manual browser testing (Step 8), creates regression tests (Step 9), validates them (Step 10), and checks for completion (Step 11).
5. **Resume Logic**: When resuming, ensure `likely_ci_cwd` is recalculated from the cached Step 12 output to ensure subsequent test execution steps run in the correct directory.
6. **Stop Conditions**:
    - If Step 1 detects a duplicate, stop.
    - If Step 3 (Clarification) returns a stop condition, save the state such that it re-runs Step 3 on resume (save `last_completed_step` as Step 2).
7. **Output Formatting**: Ensure all console output uses the `rich` library for consistent styling.

# Deliverable
A Python module named `agentic_test_orchestrator.py` containing the primary entry point `run_agentic_test_orchestrator`. The function must return a tuple: `(success: bool, message: str, total_cost: float, model: str, changed_files: List[str])`.
The entry point must accept `clean_restart: bool = False`; when true, call `clear_workflow_state(...)` before any `load_workflow_state(...)`, start from fresh in-memory state even if the clear fails, save `state["clean_restart"] = True`, post a visible Step 0 startup comment with `Mode: Clean restart`, pass the effective clean-restart flag into `_setup_worktree(...)` so stale generated branches cannot become the worktree base, and include the effective flag in the Step 17 submit-PR context so the prompt uses `git push --force-with-lease` plus existing-PR update handling. If a later normal resume loads `clean_restart=True` from state, keep using clean worktree and PR semantics.


===== agentic_test_python.prompt =====
<include>context/python_preamble.prompt</include>

% Goal
Write the `pdd/agentic_test.py` module.

% Role & Scope
CLI entry point for the agentic test generation workflow. Fetches a GitHub issue, extracts content and metadata describing what needs to be tested, then invokes the orchestrator to run the 9-step test generation process (supports UI, CLI, and API tests).

% Requirements
1. Function: `run_agentic_test(issue_url: str, *, verbose: bool = False, quiet: bool = False, timeout_adder: float = 0.0, use_github_state: bool = True, clean_restart: bool = False) -> Tuple[bool, str, float, str, List[str]]`
2. Return 5-tuple: (success, message, total_cost, model_used, changed_files)
3. Parse GitHub issue URL to extract: owner, repo, issue_number
4. Supported URL formats:
   - `https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}`
   - `https://www.github.com/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}`
   - `github.com/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}`
5. Fetch issue content via `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}` (requires `gh` CLI)
6. Extract from issue: title (as issue_title), body, labels, state, comments_url, user.login (as issue_author)
7. Fetch comments via `gh api {comments_url}` to get full context
8. Clone/checkout the repository to a temp directory if not already in it
9. Call orchestrator: `run_agentic_test_orchestrator(issue_url, issue_content, repo_owner, repo_name, issue_number, issue_author, issue_title, ..., timeout_adder=timeout_adder, use_github_state=use_github_state, clean_restart=clean_restart)`
10. Return aggregated results from orchestrator

% Manual Mode (legacy)
When `--manual` flag is used, fall back to the existing `cmd_test_main` functionality for prompt-based test generation:
- Accept positional arguments: PROMPT_FILE, CODE_OR_EXAMPLE_FILE
- Delegate to existing test generation logic for backward compatibility

% Dependencies

<pdd.agentic_common><include>context/agentic_common_example.py</include></pdd.agentic_common>
<pdd.cmd_test_main><include>context/cmd_test_main_example.py</include></pdd.cmd_test_main>

% Error Handling
- Return `(False, "gh CLI not found", 0.0, "", [])` if `gh` command not available
- Return `(False, "Invalid GitHub URL", 0.0, "", [])` if URL parsing fails
- Return `(False, "Issue not found: {error}", 0.0, "", [])` if `gh api` fails

% Deliverables
